questions.question: Explain the interrupt-driven I/O technique. How does it improve upon programmed I/O in terms of CPU efficiency?
keyPoints: CPU is free to execute other tasks while waiting for I/O device ready signal
Device initiates interrupt when ready, CPU saves context and handles the transfer
Eliminates continuous polling overhead that wastes CPU cycles
Suitable for asynchronous and relatively slow I/O devices like keyboard, mouse

questions.question: What is Direct Memory Access (DMA)? Why is DMA preferred for high-speed data transfers like disk I/O?
keyPoints: DMA controller transfers data directly between I/O device and memory without CPU intervention
CPU is freed to execute other programs during bulk data transfer
Reduces CPU overhead significantly compared to interrupt-driven I/O for large data blocks
Essential for high-speed devices where interrupt per byte would cause unacceptable overhead

questions.question: Compare programmed I/O, interrupt-driven I/O, and DMA in terms of: (i) CPU involvement during transfer, (ii) Speed and throughput, (iii) Hardware complexity and cost, and (iv) Suitable applications. Provide a practical example where each I/O method would be the optimal choice.
keyPoints: Programmed I/O: CPU busy throughout, simple hardware, suitable for slow/rare transfers like keyboard input
Interrupt-driven I/O: CPU involved at start/end, moderate hardware, good for modems and network cards with moderate data rates
DMA: CPU minimally involved, complex hardware, essential for disk drives, audio/video streaming and high-speed data acquisition
Practical examples: Programmed I/O for LED status check, Interrupt for network packet receipt, DMA for copying 5GB file from one disk to another

questions.question: A data acquisition system samples 16 analog channels at 10 kHz sampling rate with 12-bit resolution. The system needs to store continuous data to RAM for processing. (a) Explain why using programmed I/O would be impractical for this scenario. (b) If using interrupt-driven I/O, calculate the interrupt frequency assuming double buffering with 1KB buffers. (c) Justify why DMA is the most suitable I/O method for this application and describe the role of the DMA controller in this data transfer.
keyPoints: (a) Programmed I/O would require CPU to read each sample immediately, consuming 100% CPU time and making any other processing impossible
(b) Buffer size 1KB = 1024 bytes, each sample = 2 bytes, samples per buffer = 512, interrupt frequency = 10000 / 512 ≈ 20 Hz per buffer, with double buffering ~40 interrupts/sec

(c) DMA allows continuous data streaming without CPU intervention; DMA controller handles address generation and transfer completion signaling, allowing CPU to perform real-time signal processing between DMA transfers
